scenario: Financial calculation: decimal(0.125, 2) -- does it round to 0.12 or 0.13? Team wonders FEEL's rounding mode. FEEL decimal rounding mode?
question: FEEL decimal rounding mode?

options.text: The FEEL decimal function does not simply truncate toward zero for all inputs. 12 -- but FEEL applies the DMN standard rounding mode, which is banker's rounding (round half to even). Non-halfway cases round normally; only the exact halfway case differs. The FEEL spec provides explicit ound down(n, scale) for truncation when that behavior is specifically needed.

FEEL decimal(value, scale) uses banker's rounding (round half to even) per DMN spec: when the discarded value is exactly halfway, the result rounds to the nearest even digit. 14 (4 is even). This differs from common round-half-up behavior. FEEL also provides explicit round half up(n, scale) and round half down(n, scale) built-in functions for other modes. md:9-27.

The rounding mode for the FEEL decimal function is defined by the DMN specification as banker's rounding (round half to even); it is not a runtime-configurable parameter. Teams cannot pass an additional mode argument to decimal() to switch behavior. If a different rounding mode is needed -- such as round-half-up for financial reporting -- FEEL provides explicit built-in alternatives: ound half up(n, scale), ound half down(n, scale), ound up(n, scale), and ound down(n, scale).

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL decimal rounding: Banker's rounding (round half to even) per DMN spec typically: When the value is exactly halfway between two representable decimals, round to the nearest even digit. Less bias than always rounding up. Examples: `feel decimal(0.125, 2) → 0.12 (half-to-even: 2 is even) decimal(0.135, 2) → 0.14 (half-to-even: 4 is even) decimal(0.145, 2) → 0.14 (half-to-even: 4 is even) decimal(0.155, 2) → 0.16 (half-to-even: 6 is even) decimal(0.124, 2) → 0.12 (round down; not halfway) decimal(0.126, 2) → 0.13 (round up; not halfway) Other rounding modes (for context): Round half up (common in many languages): 0.125 → 0.13 (always rounds .5 up). Round half down: 0.125 → 0.12 (always rounds .5 down). Round half to even (banker's): the DMN FEEL standard. Round half to odd: rare. Round towards zero (truncate): 0.127 → 0.12. Round away from zero: 0.123 → 0.13. Why banker's rounding: Unbiased: half the time rounds up, half down (on average). Financial standard: many financial regulations require banker's rounding. DMN spec: prescribes this mode. For specific rounding modes: Round half up -- use the built-in function: feel round half up(0.125, 2) // 0.13 per feel-built-in-functions-numeric.md:155-179 round half up(-1.126, 2) // -1.13 No need for adjust-and-truncate hacks -- the FEEL spec provides explicit modes. Custom rounding in worker: For complex rounding: do in worker code (Java BigDecimal with explicit RoundingMode). Pass result back as variable. For the scenario: decimal(0.125, 2) → 0.12 (banker's rounding; 2 is even). Surprising if expecting "round half up." Pitfalls: Pitfall 1: Assuming round-half-up: Developers from other languages may expect 0.125 → 0.13. Banker's gives 0.12; surprise. Test rounding behavior explicitly. Pitfall 2: Cumulative rounding errors: Rounding many times accumulates differences. Best practice: round once, at end of calculation. Pitfall 3: Different rounding in process variables vs reports: FEEL banker's; reporting tool may use round-half-up. Reconciliation issues if not consistent. Compare with floor / ceiling: floor(value): round down to integer (truncate towards negative infinity). ceiling(value): round up to integer (truncate towards positive infinity). round(value)`: round to integer (mode depends on FEEL version). Best practices: Test rounding behavior: never assume mode. Document expected rounding: in business spec. Use decimal for final result: avoid float-point artefacts (per Set 13 Q48). Round once at end: avoid cumulative errors. For regulatory rounding: verify FEEL matches required mode; use custom logic if not.
